title: The Loaded Gun: What a Vertical Profile Reveals
subtitle: Dissolved oxygen and temperature versus depth, pre-dawn
description: A stratified water column with a survivable surface (3.1 mg/L) hiding anoxic, sulfide-generating bottom water (0.2 mg/L, negative ORP) below the thermocline at ~3.5 m. A surface sensor sees nothing wrong. A cold rain that mixes this column delivers the bottom water to the fish — the exact mechanism in the Laguna de Bay fish-kill literature. Depth is where the killing happens, and only a profile can see it.
SYNTHETIC_WARNING: FABRICATED but physically plausible profile. Describes no real station. Illustrates the stratification mechanism. Webpage MUST badge as illustrative_synthetic.
axis_rendering_rule: DEPTH ON THE Y-AXIS, INCREASING DOWNWARD. Never invert to look like a conventional chart. See dashboard-spec.json#/views (Profile view). The vertical orientation is the whole point — it makes the water column legible.

series.name: Dissolved oxygen (mg/L) — SYNTHETIC
series.note: Format is [value, depth] so depth maps to the downward Y-axis.

series.name: ORP (mV) — SYNTHETIC
series.note: Negative ORP below the thermocline signals sulfide generation — the H2S the platform infers rather than measures directly.

annotations.label: Thermocline ~3.5 m — the boundary the surface cannot see
Bottom: 0.2 mg/L, ORP -180 mV — anoxic, sulfide-generating

notes: This is the chart that justifies the reeled probe over every surface-only competitor. It should sit next to the diel chart in the pitch: one shows time-blindness, the other depth-blindness. Together they are the case for the platform.
